merger_name: Barro Group - BQC Quarries and Burdekin Concrete

acquirers.name: BQC Group Pty Ltd [yet to be incorporated]

targets.name: COLEVALE ESTATES PTY LTD & M & T POPULIN PTY LTD & TWIN ROCK PTY LTD

other_parties.name: MT. MARROW BLUE METAL QUARRIES PTY. LTD.

merger_description:
Barro Group Pty Ltd (Barro), via a subsidiary proposes to acquire by asset purchase the quarry and concrete business and specified assets (the Target) operated by Colevale Estates Pty Ltd, M&T Populin Pty Ltd and Twin Rock Pty Ltd, as the BQC Quarries partnership, and BQC Registrations Pty Ltd, BQC Sands Pty Ltd and Burdekin Concrete Pty Ltd (the Sellers) (the Acquisition).

Barro is a seller and distributor of pre-mixed concrete, quarry products and various associated products across Australia. In Northern Queensland, Barro owns and operates Townsville Concrete, which is supplied by its hard rock quarry on the Black River (north of Townsville) and also operates a sand quarry on the Haughton River (south of Townsville).

The Target assets are the Sellers’ hard rock and sand quarrying operations in Burdekin and Bowen in Northern Queensland, as well as Burdekin Concrete, which manufactures ready mix concrete in Burdekin.

Both Barro and the Target operate sand quarries, hard rock quarries, and RMX plants in the general region.

events.title: Barro - BQC Quarries and Burdekin Concrete - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Barro Group Pty Ltd (Barro) via a subsidiary proposes to acquire, by
asset purchase, the quarry and concrete business and specified
assets (the Target Assets) operated collectively by Colevale Estates
Pty Ltd, M&T Populin Pty Ltd and Twin Rock Pty Ltd as the BQC
Quarries partnership, and BQC Registrations Pty Ltd, BQC Sands Pty
Ltd and Burdekin Concrete Pty Ltd (the Sellers) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Barro is a seller and distributor of pre-mixed concrete, quarry
products and various associated products across Australia. In
Northern Queensland, Barro owns and operates Townsville Concrete,
a ready-mix concrete (RMX) plant which is supplied with hard rock
aggregates by its quarry on the Black River and coarse sand from its
sand quarry on the Haughton River.
The Target Assets are the Sellers’ hard rock and sand quarrying
operations in Burdekin and Bowen in Northern Queensland, as well
as Burdekin Concrete which manufactures RMX in Burdekin.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Overlap between the parties
Both Barro and the Sellers (the Parties) operate RMX plants, hard rock quarries and coarse sand quarries in Northern Queensland.

In Northern Queensland, Barro owns and operates Townsville Concrete, a RMX plant located in Bohle (northern Townsville). Barro’s RMX plant is supplied with hard rock products by its quarry on the Black River (north of Townsville) and coarse sand products from its sand quarry on the Haughton River (south of Townsville, in the Burdekin region of Queensland).
The Target Assets include:

items: a RMX plant in Ayr, in the Burdekin region
two hard rock quarries at Airville, in the Burdekin region, and West Euri, in the Bowen region
two sand quarries in Ayr and Mount Kelly in the Burdekin region.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).
Relevant areas of competition
The ACCC has considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition in the supply of RMX, hard rock products and coarse sand products.
The ACCC considers that while Barro and the Sellers both have a presence in Northern Queensland, the Parties operate in different geographic areas.
Competition effects in the supply of RMX
The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of RMX.

Based on information supplied by the Parties and third-party submissions, the ACCC finds that the Target Assets’ RMX plant (Burdekin Concrete) and Barro’s RMX plant (Townsville Concrete) operate in different geographic areas and therefore do not compete for the same customers.
Competition effects in the supply of hard rock products
The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of hard rock products.

The ACCC has taken into account the Parties’ own evidence on cartage costs, natural geographic boundaries, distance‑based pricing effects, and third-party submissions. Based on these materials, the ACCC finds that Barro’s and the Target Assets’ hard rock quarries operate in different geographic areas and therefore do not compete to supply the same customers.
Competition effects in coarse sand products

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of coarse sand products. The ACCC finds that there is limited horizontal overlap between Barro and BQC, and the Parties are not close competitors.

The ACCC’s view, based on information and documents provided by the Parties including evidence on cartage costs, natural geographic boundaries, distance‑based pricing effects, as well as third party submissions, is that:

items: Delivery distance is a key constraint on substitutability of coarse sand suppliers as transport costs form a significant proportion of the delivered price of coarse sand.

Due to the distance between Barro’s Haughton River quarry and the Target Assets’ Home Hill and Mount Kelly quarries, Barro and the Sellers do not compete closely to supply coarse sand products to the same customers.

As the vast majority of coarse sand produced by its Haughton River quarry is used by Barro’s own concrete manufacturing operations, Barro and the Sellers do not compete closely to supply coarse sand products to the same customers.

Although the ACCC received mixed views from some third parties regarding the alternative sand quarries in the Burdekin region, the ACCC found that there are competitors that hold sand leases located in closer proximity to the Target Assets’ Home Hill and Mount Kelly quarries.
Barro does not have the ability to foreclose rival RMX suppliers’ access to coarse sand products as rival RMX suppliers also hold sand leases in the Burdekin region.
